---
title: "A Directory of Canadian Metaresearch Initiatives"
subtitle: "Canadian Metaresearch Data Challenge — 2nd Canadian Open Science Conference"
author: "Zachary Batist, McGill University"
bibliography: references.bib
format:
  # html:
  #   theme: cosmo
  #   css: assets/styles.css
  #   toc: false
  #   number-sections: false
  #   title-block-style: default
  pdf:
      documentclass: article
      geometry: margin=0.75in
      fontsize: 11pt
      toc: false
      header-includes: |
        \usepackage{fancyhdr}
        \pagestyle{fancy}
        \usepackage{lastpage}
        \fancyhf{}
        \fancyhead[L]{A Directory of Canadian Metaresearch Initiatives}
        \fancyhead[R]{Zachary Batist, McGill University}
        \fancyfoot[R]{\thepage\ / \pageref*{LastPage}}
        \renewcommand{\headrulewidth}{0.4pt}
        \AtBeginDocument{\thispagestyle{fancy}}
        \usepackage{etoolbox}
        \makeatletter
        \patchcmd{\maketitle}{\newpage}{}{}{}
        \renewcommand{\maketitle}{}
        \makeatother
---

## Objectives

Metaresearch in Canada has no single institutional home. It is practiced under different banners, including metascience, scientometrics, science and technology studies, and science reform advocacy, and by people working across universities, libraries, granting agencies, and independent community infrastructure projects. Despite the prevalence of this kind of work, essentially no prior effort has been made to systematically document its full range and diversity across Canada. This project will fill this gap by building a directory of metaresearchers that may be used as both a discovery resource and as a dataset to better understand this vast, interdisciplinary research landscape.

This proposal draws from my experience building and maintaining [open-archaeo.info](https://open-archaeo.info/), which is a comprehensive and fairly complete listing of open source research software in the domain of archaeology, and which is used as a data resource to track developments in the field. Moreover, I have significant experience and technical expertise developing pipelines for retrieving, processing analyzing aggregated data from scholarly metadata resources (e.g., Open Alex and Crossref), from scientific papers and grey literature using machine learning techniques, from researchers' social media profiles via iterative and programmable API queries, and from unstructured web content including faculty profile pages. I will therefore be capable of producing a comprehensive and feature-complete resource within the 12-week timeframe.

## Methodology and Data Model

The directory comprises three linked data files containing information pertaining to publications, people, and initiatives. They are stored as JSON arrays and are connected by overlapping keys. The dataset is extensible and integrated with other resources on the open web such as WikiData, ORCID, RoR and OpenAlex. I have prepared a more detailed technical overviews of the data model, data sources and data processing pipeline on a dedicated project website: [zackbatist.info/CMDC](https://zackbatist.info/CMDC).

Publication records are structured as a custom extension of the BibLaTeX specification, which is commonly used to store metadata about scholarly outputs. An initial standard BibLaTex dataset will be populated by running a series of general queries using key terms pertaining to the metaresearch domains. A corpus of Canadian works will then be derived by filtering for Canadian institutions and place names in author affiliations and funding statements. For works where this information is not documented in metadata, [GROBID](https://grobid.readthedocs.io/), a machine learning tool trained to extract structured information from full-text articles, will be used. Lists of Canadian institutions and place names will be derived from WikiData or from lists of institutions authorized to receive Tri-Council research funds. Each work's title, abstract, and full text (when available) will be analyzed using machine learning and large language models to assign categories and tags in accordance with a pre-formed taxonomy and a more open-ended prompt.

People records will derive from the authors in the publications dataset. They will be populated by matching their ORCID and affiliations with additional information published on the open web, including WikiData, faculty websites and professional social media profiles (e.g., LinkedIn, Bluesky). Key terms that people use to describe their own disciplinary backgrounds and research interests will be pulled from external profiles.

Initiatives are the most challenging, but also the most rewarding aspect of the directory. A preliminary list of initiatives will be detected from faculty profile pages using a large language model prompt, which will feed into a more comprehensive refinement process to flesh out each record. Official project websites will be located and scraped to identify their missions, scopes and purview, their stated and implied methodological and disciplinary lenses, tangible and intangible outcomes deriving from their efforts, and lists of contributing members. Much of this will depend on large language models to locate, parse and make sense of relatively unstructured text, and to relate key elements embedded in the text (names of people, mentions of methods and disciplines, references to outcomes) with records already tracked in the directory. This will highlight the roles of institutional venues as mechanisms that draw research communities together and that assemble resources to support coordinated action.

A primary challenge is to balance comprehensive coverage with accurate information. Automated de-duplication and normalization processes are therefore core aspects of the pipeline. This can be achieved through relatively simple matching, for example by relating OpenAlex author identifiers with ORCIDs, and I have also had great success applying machine learning and large language model techniques to combine less structured data for the [BibVik](https://github.com/zackbatist/BibVik/tree/main/CitationAnalysis) project, which compiles a comprehensive multi-modal research network comprising tens of thousands of records. A sampled subset of disambiguation decisions will be checked by hand each run, and the estimated error rate will be reported alongside the dataset.

## Anticipated Applications and Impact

- web directory and discovery tool
- analysis of the community's diversity
- platform for advocacy and demonstration of impact
  - especially salient in the context of lack of support for interdisciplinary research in Canada, and calls to develop infrastructures that connect and leverage our distributed efforts (science advisor's data sharing strategy)

The directory supports questions a citation index answers poorly. It can show who works in this space, including people who never publish under a recognizable metaresearch label. Its relational structure supports navigation from a person to their institution and to who else is affiliated with it, or from an initiative to everyone who has contributed to it. Beyond the initial release, the directory could function as a living, updatable record for Canadian metaresearch networks such as CARN/RECAR, supporting knowledge mobilization: identifying who to invite into a conversation, who is working on adjacent problems without knowing it, or where a funder's or institution's own metaresearch capacity actually sits. This second use depends on who takes up maintaining and updating the files after October, not on any additional infrastructure beyond what is already being built for the initial deliverable.

A person's account of what they work on and what a bibliometric record shows they have published capture different things. Where they diverge, such as someone who identifies as doing STS work but whose publications get algorithmically tagged as scientometrics, that divergence is preserved as data. Publications are tagged using categories drawn from a recent paper on metascience [rubin2026sixtypes]. This is a working vocabulary, chosen so people working under different institutional banners can be found under a common set of terms. It is not treated as a settled or authoritative classification. Initiative-level relationships, meaning the community infrastructure projects, working groups, or collaborative platforms a person contributes to, are linked into the same structure. This layer will be less complete than the person and institution layers, since it depends on how well an initiative documents its own contributors.


## Openness, Transparency, and Reproducibility

Retrieval queries, inclusion and exclusion criteria, disambiguation procedures, and the tag-vocabulary mapping are published as versioned, dated artifacts in a public repository, alongside the retrieval, enrichment, and analysis code. The dataset is openly licensed. Contact information collected for internal disambiguation is not included in the public release. Full-text-derived fields are included only where source licensing permits redistribution; where it does not, only derived tags are published. A dated register of known gaps, naming populations and traditions the directory is likely under-representing, such as STS-style qualitative metaresearch, francophone scholarship, community-based and Indigenous research governance work, and practitioners like librarians and infrastructure specialists whose contributions may never be indexed, is published alongside the dataset and updated as the directory develops.

## Feasibility and Work Plan

Assuming notification on August 1, 2026 against an October 27 to 29, 2026 conference date, roughly twelve weeks are available. Retrieval, disambiguation, and enrichment, weeks 1 through 8, are the largest share of the work. The same OpenAlex and ORCID-based disambiguation approach is used in the author's other current work reconstructing citation networks (BibVik), which supports the feasibility of the core technical approach. Validation and the gaps register, weeks 8 through 10, depend on disambiguation being substantially complete. Public release and documentation, weeks 10 through 12, depend on validation being complete. Initiative-level relationships are the least certain part of the timeline, since they depend on external documentation quality outside the project's control. Deeper analysis of that layer, including funding relationships and governance structures, is treated as future work.
